# Peter Parts Branding

Use this skill when work needs to look and sound like Peter Parts instead of a generic ecommerce brand.

## When to Use

- Designing customer-facing pages, sections, or components for Peter Parts
- Writing hero copy, product copy, support copy, banners, or marketing text
- Reviewing UI concepts, landing pages, or brand applications for consistency
- Choosing colors, typography, visual hierarchy, or logo treatment
- Translating brand guidance into implementation decisions for the frontend

## Brand Intent

Peter Parts is a specialized KitchenAid parts and maintenance brand. The identity should feel expert, dependable, direct, and approachable.

The brand should read as a trusted specialist, not a broad catalog store and not a luxury fashion brand.

## Workflow

1. Identify the deliverable.
   Decide whether the task is a customer-facing interface, a marketing asset, a content/copy task, or a brand review.
2. Set the brand stance.
   Keep the output specialized, reliable, and clear. Make KitchenAid expertise legible in the visual direction or the copy.
3. Apply the core visual system.
   Use the official palette, keep contrast strong, and preserve a clean technical feel over decorative styling.
4. Apply typography by role.
   Use Open Sans for primary titles and high-attention text. Use Lato for secondary copy, descriptions, captions, and lighter communication. Do not repurpose the logo font for body or interface copy.
5. Handle the logo correctly.
   Preserve clear space, keep proportions intact, and avoid effects or busy backgrounds that weaken recognition.
6. Review tone and clarity.
   Copy should sound expert without sounding arrogant. Be direct, useful, and technically precise only when the context needs it.
7. Run the completion check.
   Confirm the output feels like a specialist brand, uses the right palette, respects logo constraints, and avoids generic marketplace language.

## Decision Rules

- If the work is customer-facing, apply the full brand system: palette, typography, tone, and logo rules.
- If the work is internal or operational, keep the tone and general palette aligned but use branding more lightly so utility stays primary.
- If the logo sits on a complex or low-contrast background, simplify the background or place the logo in a cleaner container.
- If the exact brand fonts are not available in the implementation, preserve the intended hierarchy and note the deviation instead of improvising with decorative alternatives.

## Non-Negotiables

- Use only the official brand colors for brand-defining surfaces.
- Do not stretch, compress, rotate, recolor, or decorate the logo.
- Do not place the logo on backgrounds that compromise contrast or legibility.
- Do not use the logo font for general communication text.
- Do not let the brand voice drift into generic storefront wording.

## Completion Checklist

- The output clearly feels like Peter Parts.
- The design supports a specialist KitchenAid positioning.
- The primary palette stays within the approved colors.
- The typography roles are respected.
- The logo treatment follows the safe-area and misuse rules.
- The copy is reliable, specialized, close, and careful.
- Any unavoidable deviations are explicitly called out.

## Reference

Use the cleaned brand manual for exact values and wording: [Peter Parts Visual Manual](./references/visual-manual.md)
